type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Instead of considering all the generators on campus as one large 13.7 MW generator, I split them down into two categories (> 450 kW and < = 450 kW) as instructed in the NPRI calculation spreadsheet (which I should have done the previous year, too).  The SO2 emission factor for large generators is 2.50E-02 lb/MMBTU and the one for smaller generators is 2.90E-01 lb/MMBTU.

comments.comment: Increase in the use of for emergency generators from 3,962 L in 2012 to 9,303 L in 2013.  No specific reason: use of emergency generators varies greatly from one year to the next.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Unit mistake in 2012 report.  Value that should have been reported in 2012: 0.019 kg.  Please advise whether we should revise the 2012 report.
Difference between 2012 level (0.019 kg) and 2013 level (0.024 kg): diesel consumption for emergency power generators went from 3,962 L to 9,303 L.

comments.comment: Emissions related to emergency equipment (generators) were estimated based on 24 hours of operation annually in 2010.  We have revised this number to 52 hours annually in the 2011 report to better reflect current practices at the University.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
The availability of diesel and fuel oil invoice records was limited due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Changes in methods of data retrieval and estimation were required for this submission year. Consumption data for previous submission years were pulled directly from invoices. For 2019 consumption data was pulled directly from invoices when possible, for missing invoices estimates were made from available financial data, known utility rates, and historical consumption.

In 2019 there was a significant increase in fuel consumption for generators (primarily diesel fuel). This increased generator runtime was either for emergency power or for demand response during peak electric demand events. 

In 2018 there was a computational error that resulted in 160,268 cubic meters of natural gas to be omitted from GHG and pollutant calculations (for reference this error is relatively small and represents ~1% of total natural gas consumption in 2018).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
The availability of diesel invoice records was limited due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Changes in methods of data retrieval and estimation were required for this submission year and could account for some of the large change in diesel fuel consumption observed in 2019. 

In 2019 there was a significant increase in fuel consumption for generators (primarily diesel fuel). This increased generator runtime was either for emergency power or for demand response during peak electric demand events. 

In 2018 there was a computational error that resulted in 160,268 cubic meters of natural gas to be omitted from GHG and pollutant calculations (for reference this error is relatively small and represents ~1% of total natural gas consumption in 2018).

Consumption data for previous submission years were pulled directly from invoices. For 2019 consumption data was pulled directly from invoices when possible, for missing invoices estimates were made from available financial data, known utility rates, and historical consumption.

In 2019 there was a significant increase in fuel consumption for generators (primarily diesel fuel). This increased generator runtime was either for emergency power or for demand response during peak electric demand events. 

In 2018 there was a computational error that resulted in 160,268 cubic meters of natural gas to be omitted from GHG and pollutant calculations (for reference this error is relatively small and represents ~1% of total natural gas consumption in 2018).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
The availability of diesel and fuel oil invoice records was limited due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Changes in methods of data retrieval and estimation were required for this submission year. Consumption data for previous submission years were pulled directly from invoices. For 2019 consumption data was pulled directly from invoices when possible, for missing invoices estimates were made from available financial data, known utility rates, and historical consumption.

In 2019 there was a significant increase in fuel consumption for generators (primarily diesel fuel). This increased generator runtime was either for emergency power or for demand response during peak electric demand events. 

In 2018 there was a computational error that resulted in 160,268 cubic meters of natural gas to be omitted from GHG and pollutant calculations (for reference this error is relatively small and represents ~1% of total natural gas consumption in 2018). 

Additionally, the 2019 methodologies within the calculation tools for liquid fuels provided by ECCC (Diesel fuel Generator - Fuel usage and Large stationary diesel engine (>600HP)) have changed. Although emissions factors remained the same, there are now more substances included/calculated as PAHs, therefore contributing to the increase in 2019 as more substances are now being tracked.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
We used the CEPEI Natural Gas Combustion Emissions Calculator for the 2018 Inventory Year Reporting in 2019 V Update April 16 2019 in which emission factor for natural gas boilers (in our case, NSPS Large Wall-Fired Boilers (>100 MMBtu/h Heat Input) and Small Wall-fired Boilers (<100 MMBTU/hr Heat Input)) went from 6.267E-04
lb/MMBtu in the previous calculator (2017 reporting period) to 2.320E-04 lb/MMBtu in the current version (2018 reporting period).

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Decrease in on-campus operations due to COVID, implementation of energy efficiency measures, and warmer temperatures during winter all contributed to decreased combustion of fossil fuels for heating needs. 

Some larger diesel back-up generators were replaced for units that operate on natural gas which results in fewer sulfur emissions. Additionally some diesel data in 2019 was estimated, whereas 2020 required no estimates.

reasonsForNoActivities.fr: Autres (précisez dans le champ des commentaires)

p2Plan.noActivitiesComment: The University doesn't have a pollution prevention plan. However, the University has committed to reaching carbon neutrality in all its activities by 2040. The following documents include detailed actions to reduce energy consumption and airborne contaminants and reach zero carbon emissions by 2040 : the Roadmap for the Energy Transition, the Five-Year Energy Management Plan, and the Sustainability and Climate Action Plan. None of these documents per se are pollution prevention plans, but they go beyond pollution prevention and mitigation.
